claims.quote: the alternate flowing and ebbing of the tides, which exists indeed under various forms, but is caused by the sun and the moon
First, as the moon rises the seas swell, and again they sink as she declines from the meridian; and after her setting they rise once more until she comes again beneath the earth
The tides are greatest at the new and the full moon, and least when the moon is at the half
Never are the waters moved but the moon draws them
the sun's influence is joined to hers, adding to the swelling at the conjunctions
The ocean is the companion of the moon's course: with her rising it comes on, with her setting it withdraws, and it follows her faithfully by day and by night
As the moon each day rises later, so the tide each day comes later by the like measure
Twice in the lunar day the sea is drawn up and twice let down
at the new moon and the full the springs run highest, for then the moon's power over the waters is greatest
The moon it is that lifts the waters and lets them fall
Gravity is a mutual bodily affection between cognate bodies towards union
The moon, being cognate to the waters of the earth, attracts them; and the seas are drawn toward the body of the moon by that same faculty
Were the earth to cease to attract its waters, all the sea-water would be raised up and would flow to the body of the moon
The tides, therefore, are the attraction of the waters by the moon
the moon draws them upward as she passes overhead, and so the waters heap toward her
The ebbing and flowing of the seas cannot be produced except by the motion of their basins
the Earth has two motions: the diurnal rotation upon its axis and the annual revolution about the sun
This continual alteration of speed makes the contained waters run now toward one end of their basins, now toward the other, and so they rise and fall
I hold it therefore as certain that the tides follow from the Earth's motion, and that a motionless Earth could show no tides at all
As for those who refer the cause to the moon, attributing to her some dominion over the water by an occult property, I will not give ear to such notions
The earth is surrounded and carried along by a great vortex of the subtle celestial matter, and the moon swims within this same fluid heaven
Because the space between the moon and the earth is not sufficient to let the celestial matter pass freely, that matter is pressed and its motion straitened beneath the moon
the fluid heaven presses more strongly upon the waters lying under the moon, and depresses them there, while raising the waters at the parts a quarter distant
It is not by any attraction, for I admit no attraction at a distance, but by the pressure of the subtle matter that the tides are caused
the sun, straitening the vortex likewise, augments the effect at the new and full moon
The flux and reflux of the sea arise from the actions of the sun and moon
By the theory of universal gravitation, every body attracts every other with a force proportional to the masses and inversely as the square of the distance
the sun and moon, by their gravitation, draw up the waters of the sea
The moon's force to raise the sea is greater than the sun's, being nearer the earth
When the sun and moon are in conjunction or opposition, their forces conspire, and the greatest, or spring, tides are produced
when they are in the quadratures, the sun's force takes away from the moon's, and the least, or neap, tides ensue
The heights of the tides answer in proportion to these forces, and may be computed from the masses and distances of the two luminaries
The tides are the oscillations of the sea produced by the gravitational attractions of the sun and the moon

the sea does not assume at every instant the figure of equilibrium; the waters, being fluid and possessed of inertia, respond to the periodic forces of the two bodies as a dynamical system of forced oscillations
it is not enough to compute the equilibrium tide; one must integrate the differential equations of the moving ocean under the disturbing forces, which vary with the positions of the moon and sun
The cause is the gravitation of these bodies; the true theory is the dynamical one, and from it the observed heights and times at each port may be deduced by calculation

primary_cause.gloss: The fundamental agent or process causing tides

mechanism.gloss: The physical process by which tides are generated

force_type.gloss: The kind of force involved in tidal causation

tidal_strength_pattern.gloss: When tides are strongest or weakest

sun_role.gloss: How the sun contributes to tidal effects

moon_role.gloss: Assessment of the moon's role in tides

moon_necessity.gloss: Whether the moon is required for tides

moon_tide_correspondence.gloss: How closely tide follows moon's course

tidal_delay_pattern.gloss: Timing relationship between moon's motion and tide

tidal_frequency.gloss: How many tides occur per period

earth_motions.gloss: Motions of the Earth relevant to tides

inertial_effect.gloss: Role of inertia in water movement

cosmic_medium.gloss: The substance or medium surrounding Earth

relative_force.gloss: Comparative strength of forces from different bodies

tidal_range.gloss: Variation in tidal magnitude with celestial position

tidal_height_law.gloss: Mathematical relationship of tidal height to forces

moon_driven_swelling.gloss: Direct connection of moon position to water level

earth_restraint.gloss: How Earth prevents loss of water to moon
